findings.title: Unpinned third-party action and no least-privilege permissions block
findings.description: The CI job pins actions to mutable tags rather than commit SHAs: `actions/checkout@v4` (L13) and the third-party `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@v1` (L14). A mutable tag can be re-pointed by a compromised maintainer to attacker code that then executes in CI; the third-party `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@v1` is the higher-risk one (first-party `actions/checkout` is lower risk). The workflow also declares no top-level `permissions:` block, so the GITHUB_TOKEN falls back to the repository default, which can be read-write depending on org/repo settings. The workflow triggers on `pull_request` and `push`; for fork PRs the token is read-only by default and no secrets are referenced, which limits blast radius — hence medium/low severity rather than high.
findings.recommendation: Pin actions to full commit SHAs (e.g. `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@<sha> # v1`) and add an explicit least-privilege `permissions: { contents: read }` block at the top of the workflow.

findings.title: OIDC (id-token: write) and pages: write granted workflow-wide, inherited by the build job that runs untrusted third-party code
findings.description: Permissions are declared at the workflow level — `id-token: write` and `pages: write` (L9-12) — so BOTH jobs inherit them, including the `build` job (L19-41). That build job runs a large amount of third-party/unpinned code under those privileges: `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@v1` (L24), `vp install` (arbitrary pnpm dependencies), `vp exec playwright install` and `vp run smoke` (L29-36). Only the `deploy` job (L43-51, `actions/deploy-pages@v4`) actually needs `id-token: write`/`pages: write`; the build job needs at most `contents: read`. With this over-grant, a single compromised build-time dependency or the unpinned setup action could mint OIDC tokens (usable against any cloud trust configured for this repo) and/or publish arbitrary content to the project's GitHub Pages site (defacement / malware under the project domain). The workflow only triggers on push-to-main and workflow_dispatch (write-access required), which bounds who can trigger it and keeps this at MEDIUM rather than HIGH.
findings.recommendation: Set workflow-level `permissions: { contents: read }` and grant `id-token: write` + `pages: write` only on the `deploy` job (job-level permissions). Keep the build job at read-only so compromised build dependencies cannot reach OIDC or Pages publishing.

findings.title: Actions pinned to mutable tags instead of commit SHAs
findings.description: All actions are referenced by mutable major tags: `actions/checkout@v4` (L22), third-party `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@v1` (L24), `actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3` (L39), `actions/deploy-pages@v4` (L51). Because this workflow holds `id-token: write` and `pages: write` (see the permissions finding), a re-pointed tag — most concerning for the third-party `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@v1` — would run attacker code with OIDC/Pages publishing capability. Pinning to immutable commit SHAs removes the moved-tag supply-chain vector.
findings.recommendation: Pin every action to a full commit SHA with a trailing version comment (e.g. `voidzero-dev/setup-vp@<sha> # v1`), and enable Dependabot for github-actions to keep the pins current.

findings.title: Public '/' and '/cart' routes expose all users' order history (no guard)
findings.description: commerceHomeRoute ('/') and commerceCartRoute ('/cart') (lines 77-99) have no `guard` and render renderCartPageBody(request.db, ...) (lines 84, 96), which embeds renderOrderHistory(db) — the unscoped list of every user's orders (see app.ts finding). Because the app uses per-declaration auth with no front-of-handler middleware, these routes are fully public, so any unauthenticated request to '/' or '/cart' returns all tenants' order records (productId, qty, total, userId). The same routes are also the delivery surface for the unescaped order-history fragment (stored-XSS sink). The static-export shell renders the same order-history fragment (lines 127-153), though against a build-time db. Contrast with the guarded orderCsvRoute/attachmentDownloadRoute (app.ts:534,545), which both require auth and filter by session user.
findings.recommendation: Either gate the cart/home routes' order-history section behind betterAuthAuthed and render only the session user's orders, or remove order history from the public storefront page entirely. Ensure renderCartPageBody only emits order history for an authenticated, scoped session.

findings.title: Enhanced sign-in redirect uses unvalidated `next` target
findings.description: The app-shell's mutationResponse for commerceSignIn returns redirectTo = authRedirectTo(result.value) (lines 184, 342-349), and result.value.redirectTo is the output of @jiso/better-auth redirectPath(input.next, '/cart'). As detailed in the app.ts open-redirect finding, redirectPath rejects '//evil.com' but not the backslash form '/\evil.com', which browsers normalize to '//evil.com' → cross-origin redirect. Because the production commerce server wires sign-in through this mutationResponse, the open redirect is reachable on the live login flow, not just the no-JS helper. nextFromRawInput (lines 328-340) likewise reflects the raw `next` into the failure-page login form.
findings.recommendation: Fix the shared root cause in better-auth's redirectPath (reject backslashes / require same-origin). Do not rely on escapeAttribute in the form — it does not neutralize the redirect payload.

findings.title: Order history renders all users' orders (no per-user scoping)
findings.description: renderOrderHistory(db) (lines 685-689) passes db.orders — the GLOBAL list of every user's orders — to the OrderHistory component, with no filter by the authenticated user. The same unscoped read exists in orderHistoryQuery (queries.ts:30-35: read('orders')). This fragment is embedded by renderCartPageBody (line 771) and served on the public '/' and '/cart' routes (app-shell.ts:84,96), which carry NO guard. As a result, any unauthenticated visitor sees every user's order records: id, productId, quantity, total, and the owning userId. This is a cross-tenant information disclosure. The app demonstrates the intended per-user scoping elsewhere: ordersCsvStream filters `order.userId === userId` (lines 707-708) and attachmentDownloadRoute filters `item.userId === request.session.user.id` (line 548) — the order-history path simply omits that filter. Severity could be argued higher given it is fully unauthenticated and exposes all tenants at once.
findings.recommendation: Scope order history to the authenticated user: filter db.orders by request.session.user.id (mirroring ordersCsvStream), and only render the order-history fragment for authenticated sessions. Apply the same filter in orderHistoryQuery's load().

findings.title: Open redirect via login `next` param (backslash bypass of redirectPath)
findings.description: The login form embeds a user-controlled `next` value (line 839, sourced from ?next= in app-shell.ts:107) and submitCommerceSignInNoJs returns redirectTo = result.value.redirectTo (line 870), which the no-JS handler emits as a literal HTTP `Location` header (server/mutation.ts:639-642). result.value.redirectTo is computed by redirectPath(input.next, '/cart') in @jiso/better-auth (index.ts:962). redirectPath only does: `if (!value.startsWith('/') || value.startsWith('//')) return fallback;` (index.ts:1204-1209). It rejects protocol-relative `//evil.com` but NOT backslash variants: `next=/\evil.com` starts with '/' and does not start with '//', so it passes through unchanged. Browsers normalize backslashes to forward slashes per the WHATWG URL spec, so `Location: /\evil.com` resolves to `https://evil.com` — an off-site redirect. A victim who clicks https://victim/login?next=/\evil.com and signs in is bounced to the attacker's site (phishing). The existing test only covers the `https://...` case (better-auth index.test.ts:2462), missing the backslash bypass. The enhanced path is affected identically via authRedirectTo (app-shell.ts:342-349).
findings.recommendation: Harden redirectPath to accept only same-site path-absolute targets: reject any value whose second character is '/' or '\\', reject values containing ':' or '\\', or parse with `new URL(value, base)` and require the resolved origin to equal the app origin. Add a test for `next=/\evil.com` and `next=/\/evil.com`.

findings.title: Sign-in mutation has no rate limiting (credential brute force)
findings.description: commerceSignIn (lines 247-253) is created with only `csrf` and `defaultRedirectTo` — it has no `guard`, so no guards.rateLimit is applied to the credential check. Sibling state-changing mutations DO rate-limit: addToCart uses guards.rateLimit({max:10, per:'session'}) (line 396) and uploadReceipt uses {max:5} (line 456). Because auth is per-declaration with no global middleware (per project design), the sign-in endpoint accepts unlimited password attempts, enabling brute force / password spraying against the credential store. CSRF does not mitigate this: for the pre-session login the CSRF sessionId falls back to the constant authCsrfId 'commerce-shell-login' (app-shell.ts:289) HMAC'd with a hardcoded example secret, so the token is static and reusable across attempts (and an attacker can simply fetch a fresh token from /login per attempt).
findings.recommendation: Add a rate-limit guard to commerceSignIn keyed by IP and/or submitted email, e.g. guard: guards.rateLimit({ max: 5, per: 'ip' }) (composed via guards.all if other guards are added), plus account lockout/backoff on repeated failures.

findings.title: Stored XSS sink: order `productId` rendered into a fragment without HTML escaping
findings.description: renderOrderHistory (lines 685-689) renders each order via the OrderHistory component, which emits `{item.productId}` as a JSX text child (generated/order-history.tsx:20-21). The server JSX runtime escapes ATTRIBUTES but NOT text children: renderJsxChildren returns `String(children)` verbatim (server/jsx-runtime.ts:79-84, with a comment acknowledging text-escaping is undefined), and the fragment wrapper inserts this HTML raw (server/wire-html.ts:64). Per the project threat model these fragments are applied client-side via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML, so an unescaped interpolation is stored XSS. addToCart cannot inject arbitrary productId (it is constrained to existing product keys by the `request.db.products.get` check at line 407), but the payment webhook handler writes orders with attacker-shaped data: `productId: paid.productId` straight from the event body (line 516, schema is just s.string()). Combined with the hardcoded webhook secret (line 355, see separate finding) an attacker can forge a valid Stripe signature, write an order whose productId is `` for ANY userId (line 521), and have it executed when the victim loads the cart page or receives the order-history fragment after an add-to-cart. The webhook is also the project's #4 ranked threat surface.
findings.recommendation: HTML-escape all DB/user strings rendered as fragment text. Fix the JSX runtime to escape text children (renderJsxChildren) so components are safe by default, or have the order-history component wrap interpolations in escapeHtml. Defense-in-depth: validate webhook productId against known products before persisting.

findings.title: Receipt storage key derived from unscoped filename → cross-user overwrite/disclosure
findings.description: uploadReceipt stores the file at key `receipts/${file.name}` (lines 448-452) — derived solely from the attacker-controlled upload filename, with no namespacing by user id or attachment id. createMemoryStorage.put overwrites any existing object at the same key (core/storage.ts put → objects.set). So if user A and user B both upload `receipt.pdf`, the second write overwrites the first's bytes, while both users' attachment records still point at the same storageKey. attachmentDownloadRoute authorizes by attachment.userId (line 548) but then streams found.storageKey — so after a collision, a user downloading their own attachment receives the OTHER user's file content. This is a cross-tenant content disclosure (opportunistic, on filename collision) plus a deterministic data-integrity bug (silent overwrite). NOTE: latent today — uploadReceipt is not registered in the app shell's mutations list (app-shell.ts:249) and is fail-closed on CSRF (no csrf config → default-on rejects it, mutation.ts:268), so it is not currently reachable over the wire; the flaw is in the handler/key design and would activate if wired.
findings.recommendation: Namespace storage keys with non-collidable, server-controlled components, e.g. `receipts/${userId}/${attachmentId}/${sanitize(file.name)}`, and sanitize file.name (strip path separators) to also prevent path traversal on filesystem-backed storage.

findings.title: uploadReceipt trusts user-supplied orderId without ownership/existence check
findings.description: uploadReceipt's handler writes an attachment with `orderId: input.orderId` (lines 466-474) taken directly from user input, with no verification that the order exists or that it belongs to the authenticated user (currentSession.user.id). An authenticated user can attach a receipt referencing any other user's orderId. Current impact is limited because attachments are read back filtered by userId (so cross-user READ is blocked) and no code authorizes anything off attachment.orderId — but it is a missing ownership check that violates the project's stated IDOR pattern (look up by id must also filter by session user) and would become exploitable if any feature later renders/acts on receipts by orderId. Latent for the same wiring reason as the storage-key finding (uploadReceipt not registered, fail-closed on CSRF).
findings.recommendation: Before writing the attachment, verify the order exists and is owned by the session user: `request.db.orders.find(o => o.id === input.orderId && o.userId === currentSession.user.id)`, returning a failure otherwise.

findings.title: Hardcoded Stripe webhook signing secret in source
findings.description: commercePaymentWebhookSecret = 'whsec_commerce_reference_app' is hardcoded (line 355) and is the only thing protecting the payment webhook (line 488). Anyone with read access to this (public example) source can forge a valid signature and reach the webhook handler, which writes orders for arbitrary userId/total/productId (lines 516-522) — enabling order fraud and the stored-XSS chain described above. This appears to follow the same example-fixture convention as the documented EXAMPLE_ONLY_*_CSRF_SECRET / commerceAuthUsers stubs, so in the reference app it is likely intentional; however, unlike those, it is not in the documented false-positive allowlist, and the identical code shape in a real deployment is a HIGH-severity secret exposure that defeats webhook authentication.
findings.recommendation: Load the webhook secret from an environment variable / secret manager (e.g. process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET) with no hardcoded fallback, and document it as example-only if it must remain in the reference app.

findings.title: Latent stored XSS: DB-sourced product id rendered as unescaped JSX child in a fragment applied via innerHTML
findings.description:
The @jiso/server JSX runtime (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts) escapes attribute values via escapeAttribute (renderJsxAttributes, L66) but inserts text CHILDREN raw via renderJsxChildren -> String(children) (L79-84), an explicitly-documented 'open question'. In product-grid.tsx, renderProductCard renders `

{item.id}

` (L92-93) where item.id is DB data from loadProductGrid -> db.read('products'). The product grid is a fragmentTarget; its markup is delivered as a server fragment and applied on the client via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML (per CLAUDE.md threat model item 3 and runtime/response-fragment-apply.ts / morph.ts). Per the framework's own contract (CLAUDE.md: 'Server fragment renderers must run user/DB strings through escapeHtml/escapeAttribute ... an unescaped interpolation is stored XSS'), DB strings are untrusted for XSS. NOTE ON EXPLOITABILITY: in the current reference app this is NOT exploitable because product ids are server-seeded ('p1'/'p2'/'p3' in createCommerceDb, app.ts L270) and the only product write path (addToCart, app.ts L424) decrements stock on an already-looked-up existing product, so no code path lets an attacker introduce a product id containing '
findings.recommendation:
Do not rely on the data source being benign — encode at the sink. Either (a) make the @jiso/server JSX runtime escape text children (renderJsxChildren should HTML-escape String(children) for non-pre-rendered nodes, with an explicit raw/unsafe opt-in for composed pre-rendered HTML), closing the framework-wide gap; or (b) until that lands, wrap DB/user string interpolations in escapeHtml() inside fragment renderers, e.g. `

{escapeHtml(item.id)}

`. Regenerate the lowered IR (src/generated/product-grid.tsx) after the fix and add a test asserting a product id containing HTML metacharacters is escaped in the rendered fragment.

findings.title: Open redirect reachable through the enhanced shell sign-in flow
findings.description: Same open-redirect class as in app.ts, via the shell's interactive path. The /login route reads `context.search.next` straight from the query string (L75) and seeds it into the login form, and createReferenceAppShell wires the sign-in mutation response with `redirectTo: (result) => authRedirectTo(result.value)` (L86, L106). authRedirectTo (L180-187) returns `result.value.redirectTo` verbatim, which is the better-auth `redirectPath(input.next, ...)` output. Because redirectPath (packages/better-auth/src/index.ts:1204-1209) blocks `//` but not a leading `/\`, a link such as `/login?next=/\evil.com` causes the shell's request handler to emit `Location: /\evil.com`, which browsers normalize to `https://evil.com` after the victim authenticates. The scanner's xss flags (L62 controlled client-module href; L76 next is escapeAttribute-escaped in the form; L112/L132 routeValueToHtml wrapping) and insecure-crypto flags (L53/L71 are meta description/title strings) are all false positives.
findings.recommendation: Fix redirectPath upstream to reject backslash-prefixed and control-character values (see app.ts recommendation), and/or validate `context.search.next` against a same-origin path allowlist in the /login route before passing it into the form and redirect.

findings.title: Open redirect via `next` parameter (backslash bypass of redirect allowlist)
findings.recommendation: Harden redirectPath to require an exact single leading slash and reject backslashes and control characters, e.g. reject when `!/^\/[^/\\]/.test(value)` (also normalize/parse with a strict same-origin check). Alternatively, validate `next` against an explicit allowlist of known internal paths before using it as a redirect target.

findings.title: Unescaped session user fields interpolated into route page HTML
findings.description: accountRoute.page returns `account:${request.session.user.email}...` (L255) and adminRoute.page returns `admin:${request.session?.user.id ...}...` (L262) by raw template-literal interpolation. Unlike JSX component output (which the compiler auto-escapes), route `page()` handlers return raw HTML strings that the framework does not escape before sending to the browser, so any HTML/script in these session fields would execute (stored XSS on navigation). In the shipped reference app the user store is a fixed in-memory map (ada@/grace@example.com, ids u1/u2), so this is not exploitable as-is — the values are trusted/internal. It is reported at low confidence as a latent issue because this is canonical reference/example code: Better Auth's own betterAuthSignUpEmailMutation accepts attacker-controlled `email` and `name`, so an app following this pattern with real registration would render attacker-controlled strings unescaped. This is exactly the 'unescaped user/DB data into server-rendered HTML' threat the framework warns about.
findings.recommendation: Run session-derived strings through escapeHtml (packages/server/src/html.ts) before interpolating into route HTML, e.g. `account:${escapeHtml(request.session.user.email)}`, and treat all session/DB strings as untrusted in page handlers the same way JSX does.

findings.title: Open redirect in redirectPath: backslash bypass of the protocol-relative URL check
findings.description: betterAuthSignInEmailMutation (L944-964) and betterAuthSignUpEmailMutation (L998-1019) compute their post-auth destination as `redirectTo: redirectPath(input.next, options.defaultRedirectTo ?? '/')`. The `next` value is fully attacker-controlled: it is declared on betterAuthSignInEmailInput (L154-158) / betterAuthSignUpEmailInput (L160-165) and arrives as a query param or form field (e.g. `/login?next=...`). The resulting `result.value.redirectTo` is emitted verbatim as a 303 `Location:` header by consumers (confirmed in examples/reference/src/app.ts:342 and 374, and app-shell.ts authRedirectTo). The only validation is redirectPath (L1204-1209): `if (!value.startsWith('/') || value.startsWith('//')) return fallback;`. This blocks the protocol-relative form `//evil.com` but NOT the slash-backslash form `/\evil.com`. Per the WHATWG URL spec, browsers normalize backslashes to forward slashes for special (http/https) schemes, so `Location: /\evil.com` is parsed as `//evil.com`, a scheme-relative URL that navigates to `https://evil.com/`. Attack: send a victim `https://victim/login?next=/%5Cevil.com` (or a pre-filled form with `next=/\evil.com`); after a successful sign-in the browser is redirected off-site to the attacker, enabling phishing/credential-harvesting that appears to originate from the trusted login flow. Because redirectPath is a framework-provided helper used internally by the blessed credential mutations, every app relying on the better-auth adapter's `next` redirect inherits this bypass. (Related: redirectPath also returns control characters such as CRLF unchanged when they follow a leading '/', though the Headers layer typically rejects those.)
findings.recommendation: Treat backslashes as slashes before validating, and require the path to start with '/' followed by a non-slash/non-backslash char. E.g. `if (!/^\/[^/\\]/.test(value)) return fallback;` and additionally reject any value containing control characters (\r, \n, \t) or '\\'. More robustly, resolve against a known base with `new URL(value, base)` and confirm the resulting origin equals the app origin before using it.

findings.title: Credential mutations treat any non-{400,401,403} better-auth response as a successful sign-in
findings.description: In the sign-in (L946-964) and sign-up (L1000-1019) handlers, failure is detected only via isBetterAuthCredentialFailureResponse -> isCredentialFailureStatus, which returns true exclusively for HTTP 400/401/403 (L1192-1194). Every other status falls through to `forwardBetterAuthSetCookie(...)` and returns `{ status: 'signed-in'/'signed-up' }` with a redirect. So a 429 (rate-limited), 5xx (better-auth/internal error), or a 200 two-factor-pending response (better-auth returns 200 with a twoFactorRedirect body and no full session cookie when 2FA is required) is reported to the app as a successful sign-in and triggers the post-auth redirect. This is not a direct auth bypass because access still requires the session cookie that better-auth only issues on genuine success, but it produces seriously misleading behavior: users are redirected as 'signed-in' on transient failures, and apps with 2FA enabled would advance past the 2FA UI step. The success path should be positively confirmed, not inferred from the absence of three specific error codes.
findings.recommendation: Detect success positively (e.g. require response.status in the 2xx range AND a session-establishing Set-Cookie) rather than treating 'not 400/401/403' as success. Explicitly handle rate-limit/5xx (surface an error to the user) and the two-factor-pending 200 case instead of redirecting as fully signed-in.

findings.title: Filesystem storage sidecar suffix collides with the object-key namespace, corrupting another object's metadata
findings.description: createFileSystemStorage persists each object's metadata to a sidecar file at `${blobPath}.jiso-storage.json` (metadataFilePath, L418-420; sidecarSuffix L103). The suffix is not reserved: a key ending in `.jiso-storage.json` passes normalizeStorageKey unchanged (it contains no empty/'.'/'..' segments and no null byte/leading slash), so it resolves to a real blob path. Consequence: writing object `a` (put('a')) creates `<root>/a` and `<root>/a.jiso-storage.json`; later writing object `a.jiso-storage.json` (put('a.jiso-storage.json')) writes its blob bytes straight onto `<root>/a.jiso-storage.json` — which IS object `a`'s metadata sidecar. The two keys interfere bidirectionally. After such a write, fileSystemStat('a') (L389-394) reads the clobbered sidecar and runs JSON.parse on attacker-supplied blob bytes; the surrounding .catch only swallows ENOENT and rethrows everything else, so a JSON parse error propagates and get('a')/stat('a')/stream('a') all reject — a read denial-of-service plus metadata (contentType/etag/metadata) corruption for a different key than the one written. This is deterministic (I traced the path construction), not a heuristic guess. Security relevance is conditional on the app exposing user-controlled, non-namespaced storage keys: in that configuration one tenant can break another tenant's object reads by uploading to the colliding `<victimKey>.jiso-storage.json` key. The memory and S3 adapters do not use sidecars and are unaffected.
findings.recommendation: Remove the overlap between the metadata namespace and the object namespace. Options: (1) store metadata out-of-band (a separate sibling directory tree, e.g. `<root>/.meta/<key>.json`, or a single index file) rather than as an in-tree sidecar; (2) reject keys whose final segment ends with the reserved sidecar suffix inside normalizeStorageKey (or a storage-specific guard); and/or (3) make fileSystemStat tolerate an unparseable/foreign sidecar by treating JSON.parse failures the same as a missing record (fall back to filesystem stat) instead of rethrowing, so a corrupt sidecar cannot turn into a hard read failure.

findings.title: Hardcoded default CSRF secret ships verbatim into every scaffolded app
findings.description: starterAuthCsrf.secret is the literal 'replace-with-a-deployed-secret' (L59). This value is the HMAC-SHA256 key used to mint and validate ALL CSRF tokens for the scaffolded app: csrfToken/validateCsrfToken in packages/server/src/csrf.ts compute createHmac('sha256', options.secret).update(sessionId). I confirmed the create-jiso scaffolder does NOT replace it: renderTemplate (packages/create-jiso/src/index.ts:125) only substitutes {{mustache}} tokens, and this secret is a plain string literal, so it is copied verbatim. There is no process.env read, no generated random secret, no .env.example, and neither templates/README.md nor templates/docs/deployment.md mentions replacing it. Because the value is published in the create-jiso package, it is attacker-known. The stateless synchronizer-token design relies on the HMAC key being secret; with a public key the scheme's security collapses to the secrecy/predictability of the session id (request.session.id) or, for the unauthenticated login form, request.authCsrfId. If a session id is ever exposed (logs, URLs, API payloads) or authCsrfId is predictable/attacker-influenceable, an attacker can compute a valid token = HMAC-SHA256('replace-with-a-deployed-secret', id) and forge CSRF requests, defeating the protection on the sign-in and sign-out mutations that reference starterAuthCsrf. Note: app-shell.ts already demonstrates the correct pattern of reading deploy config from process.env.
findings.recommendation: Do not hardcode the secret. Read it from the environment and fail closed if unset, e.g. `const secret = process.env.JISO_CSRF_SECRET; if (!secret) throw new Error('JISO_CSRF_SECRET is required')`. Alternatively, have the create-jiso scaffolder generate a unique random secret per project (crypto.randomBytes) and write it to a generated .env, and document the requirement in deployment.md/README.

findings.title: Login open redirect via `next` (backslash bypass of redirect validation)
findings.description: The starter login recipe renders an attacker-controllable `next` value into the sign-in form (renderLoginForm L119: <input type="hidden" name="next" value={options.next ?? '/cart'} />) and configures the sign-in mutation with defaultRedirectTo (L81). On submit, `next` is parsed by betterAuthSignInEmailInput (next: optionalStringSchema, which only checks 'is a string') and passed to redirectPath(input.next, options.defaultRedirectTo ?? '/') in the sign-in handler. The returned value becomes the HTTP `Location:` header of a 303 redirect (confirmed: packages/server/src/mutation.ts:639-646 sets Location from redirectTo). The validator redirectPath (packages/better-auth/src/index.ts:1204-1209) rejects values not starting with '/' and rejects values starting with '//', but it does NOT reject a leading backslash: `/\evil.com` passes (startsWith('/') is true, startsWith('//') is false). Per the WHATWG URL spec, browsers normalize backslashes to forward slashes in the authority position, so `Location: /\evil.com` resolves to the scheme-relative `//evil.com` -> https://evil.com. An attacker can send a victim a link like /login?next=/\evil.com (the route handler populating options.next from the query string); after the victim authenticates, they are redirected off-site, enabling phishing/credential or token theft. The root-cause fix belongs in redirectPath, but the starter recipe is the reachable, user-facing surface.
findings.recommendation: Harden the redirect allowlist used by the sign-in/sign-up mutations (redirectPath in @jiso/better-auth): after confirming a leading '/', also reject values whose second character is '/' OR '\\' (and reject control/whitespace and encoded variants), or parse with `new URL(next, base)` and require the resolved origin to equal the app origin. Prefer an explicit path allowlist for post-login destinations.

refusal.reason: Analyzed all five candidate files fully. scratch/compiler-lowering.html was read in full but treated as out-of-scope only after confirming via git check-ignore that it is gitignored/untracked (and it is static documentation with escaped example code).
refusal.raw:
```json
{
  "refused": false,
  "reason": "Analyzed all five candidate files fully. scratch/compiler-lowering.html was read in full but treated as out-of-scope only after confirming via git check-ignore that it is gitignored/untracked (and it is static documentation with escaped example code).",
  "skipped": []
}
```

findings.title: searchParamsToRecord assigns attacker-controlled query-param names as object keys (localized prototype reassignment)
findings.description: searchParamsToRecord() builds the route/query `search` bag by doing `record[key] = value` / `record[key] = [existing, value]` where `key` is the raw query-parameter name (fully attacker-controlled) and `record` is a plain `{}`. A request like `?__proto__=x` makes `record['__proto__']` read back Object.prototype (truthy, non-array), so the code takes the else branch and assigns `record['__proto__'] = [Object.prototype, 'x']`, an object value — which invokes the inherited `__proto__` setter and REASSIGNS the prototype of the `search` object to that array. This is NOT global prototype pollution (single-level assignment cannot reach Object.prototype, and `?__proto__[x]=y` is parsed as a literal key), so impact is bounded to the per-request `search` object. However, that object is handed to route/query handlers: its prototype chain now includes Array.prototype/the array, so inherited reads (`search.length` === 2, `search[0]`, array methods) and `for..in` enumeration (yields '0','1') are corrupted, which can confuse handlers that inspect arbitrary/derived keys. It is a genuine tainted-key-assignment defect; I am classifying it BUG rather than a security severity because it does not achieve cross-object/global pollution.
findings.recommendation: Use a null-prototype accumulator (`const record = Object.create(null)`) or a Map so user-controlled keys cannot touch `__proto__`/`constructor` accessors. Alternatively guard against `key === '__proto__'` (and use Object.hasOwn for the existing-value check). A null-prototype object also removes spurious inherited properties from the `search` bag.

findings.title: Default mutation redirect derives the 303 Location from an unvalidated Referer header
findings.description: On the no-JS POST-redirect-GET path, a successful mutation returns a 303 whose `Location` is `endpointRequest.redirectTo`, which `handleAppMutationRequest` defaults to `defaultMutationRedirectTo(mutationRequest, currentUrl)` (L62-63). That helper reads the attacker-influenceable `Referer` request header and: (1) in its catch branch returns the **raw, unvalidated `referer` string** verbatim (L88-89) — if it is a protocol-relative value like `//evil.com` or `/\evil.com` (which `new URL()` rejects, hitting the catch), it becomes the `Location` of the 303 redirect; and (2) in the happy path returns `appRequestUrl(url)` = `${pathname}${search}${hash}` (app-document.ts:90-92), which does NOT strip a `//host` pathname (`new URL('https://victim.com//evil.com').pathname === '//evil.com'`), so a `//host` value still passes through as a protocol-relative redirect. This is the same-origin redirect guard that the framework already implements correctly elsewhere — better-auth's `redirectPath` rejects any value that does not start with `/` or that starts with `//` (better-auth/src/index.ts:1204-1207) — so this default is inconsistent with the framework's own pattern. Practical exploitability is limited (hence low confidence): the `Location` is only emitted on the no-JS success path, which requires a valid session-bound CSRF token (so the form must be same-origin), and real browsers always send an absolute URL as `Referer`, which normalizes to a same-origin path — so the dangerous catch/`//host` cases are not reachable via an ordinary victim browser flow. It is nonetheless a real latent open-redirect defect: any caller, proxy/referrer-policy rewrite, or future reuse of `defaultMutationRedirectTo` that yields a malformed or `//`-prefixed value produces an attacker-controlled redirect target.
findings.recommendation: Do not return the raw `referer`. Reuse the framework's existing same-origin guard (as in better-auth `redirectPath`): require the resolved redirect to be a string that starts with a single '/' and not '//' (and not '/\'), otherwise fall back to a safe default such as `currentUrl` or '/'. Apply the same check to the `appRequestUrl(url)` result so a `//host` pathname cannot pass through, and drop the catch-branch `return referer;` in favor of the fallback.

findings.title: Protocol-relative open redirect via trailing-slash path normalization
findings.description: handleAppRequest() (the production dispatcher returned by createRequestHandler / app.ts:76, SPEC §9.5) builds a 308 redirect directly from the normalized request pathname with no validation that the target is a safe same-origin path: `url.pathname = match.normalization.redirect.pathname; ... headers: { Location: `${url.pathname}${url.search}${url.hash}` }`. The pathname is taken straight from `new URL(request.url).pathname` and normalized by normalizePathname() in match.ts, which removes a trailing slash but does NOT collapse or reject leading double-slashes. Attack: a victim is lured to `https://victim.com//evil.com/` (path = `//evil.com/`, host still victim.com). normalizePathname strips the trailing slash, yielding redirect target `//evil.com`; because it differs from the input it returns a 308. app-request.ts then emits `Location: //evil.com`. A `//host` Location is a protocol-relative URL, so the browser navigates to `https://evil.com`. (Backslash variants like `/\evil.com/` are converted to `//evil.com/` by the URL parser and work identically.) This requires no auth, no CSRF token, and no session — purely a crafted link — enabling phishing, OAuth/redirect-flow token theft, and bypass of referer-based protections. Note: the trailing slash is what triggers the 308; `//evil.com` with no trailing slash does not redirect.
findings.recommendation: Before emitting the Location, guarantee the redirect target is a single-origin path: reject or collapse any pathname that begins with `//` (or `/\`). E.g. normalize leading slashes to a single `/` in normalizePathname, or in app-request.ts assert `redirect.pathname.startsWith('/') && !redirect.pathname.startsWith('//')` and 404/400 otherwise. Building the Location via `new URL(path, requestOrigin)` and re-extracting only pathname+search+hash would also neutralize the protocol-relative form.

findings.title: Guard returning null is treated as 'allow' (fail-open asymmetry)
findings.description: runGuard() and guards.all() use the sentinel `null` to mean 'no failure / allowed' (runGuard returns `GuardFailure | null`). When a guard returns a non-`true` value, it is normalized via guardFailureFromResult() (L266-274): `if (typeof result === 'object') return result;`. Because `typeof null === 'object'`, a guard result of `null` is returned unchanged, and that `null` is then interpreted downstream as 'allowed'. Trace: guards.all() (L132-134) does `if (result !== true) return guardFailureFromResult(result)` so a member guard returning null makes the whole composed guard resolve to null; runGuard() (L219-220) does `result === true ? null : guardFailureFromResult(result)` so a guard returning null makes runGuard return null = pass. This is asymmetric and dangerous: a guard that returns `undefined` fails CLOSED (typeof undefined !== 'object', so it becomes the generic UNAUTHORIZED 422), but a guard that returns `null` fails OPEN. A custom app guard that yields null on a deny path (e.g. `return lookup() // null on miss`, or a loosely/any-typed guard `req => req.session?.user ? true : null`) would silently bypass authorization. Built-in guards never return null, so this is a latent footgun rather than a live bypass, but the consequence when triggered is a full auth/authorization bypass on the protected declaration.
findings.recommendation: Treat ONLY `true` as allow and coerce everything else (including null and non-GuardFailure objects) to a denial. In guardFailureFromResult, gate on a positive shape check, e.g. `if (result && typeof result === 'object' && 'code' in result) return result as GuardFailure;` and fall through to the generic UNAUTHORIZED failure for null/undefined/malformed values.

findings.title: FIFO eviction of the bounded rate-limit map allows counter reset
findings.description: guards.rateLimit keeps an in-memory `counts` Map bounded by maxKeys (default 10,000). When a new key is inserted and the map is full, it evicts the OLDEST entry by insertion order (L166-170: `counts.keys().next().value` then delete), i.e. FIFO, not LRU. Crucially, an existing key's counter is incremented in place (`existing.count += 1`, L161) which does NOT move it to the back of the Map's insertion order. Therefore an actively rate-limited victim's counter is among the OLDEST and is the first to be evicted. An attacker who can generate maxKeys distinct keys within the window flushes existing counters out of the map; on the next request for an evicted key, `counts.get(key)` is undefined and a fresh `{count: 1}` window is created (L172-176), resetting the limit. Exploitability depends on key controllability: with the default/`per:'session'` keying it requires many distinct sessions (e.g. ~10k accounts), but for any endpoint using a custom `key` derived from request data (e.g. an IP/header), an attacker can cheaply mint distinct keys and reset their own throttle. The example app relies on guards.rateLimit for abuse protection on state-changing mutations (addToCart max:10, uploadReceipt max:5), so a reset undermines that protection.
findings.recommendation: Avoid eviction strategies that can be flushed by an attacker: shard/limit keyspace per identity, evict by soonest-resetAt rather than insertion order, or move to a durable/atomic store (e.g. Redis with per-key TTL) for rate limiting. At minimum, do not allow new-key insertion to evict unexpired counters belonging to other identities.

findings.title: All unauthenticated clients collapse into a single global 'anonymous' rate-limit bucket
findings.description: rateLimitKey() (L389-397) falls back to `request.session?.id ?? request.session?.user?.id ?? 'anonymous'` when no custom `key` is supplied and `per` is not 'global'. For any endpoint that applies guards.rateLimit WITHOUT also requiring authentication (and without a custom key), every unauthenticated client shares the single literal key 'anonymous'. Consequences: (1) Availability/DoS — one client can exhaust the shared bucket (e.g. send the first `max` requests), locking out all other anonymous users for the window; on a public, sensitive, or expensive (LLM/paid-API) endpoint this is an easy denial-of-service / collateral lockout. (2) No per-client throttling exists for anonymous traffic, so the limit cannot distinguish abusers from legitimate users. This is a default-behavior footgun rather than a code defect, but it is easy to misuse because the rate limiter appears to provide per-client protection.
findings.recommendation: Require an explicit `key` (or refuse to apply per-client semantics) when the request is unauthenticated, or default the anonymous key to a connection identifier the app explicitly designates (validated client IP) rather than a single shared constant. Document that guards.rateLimit on unauthenticated endpoints needs a custom key.

findings.title: JSX runtime inserts text children unescaped (stored XSS by default)
findings.description:
The server JSX runtime escapes attribute values (renderJsxAttributes uses escapeAttribute, L66) but renders element/text children completely raw: renderJsxChildren (L79-84) returns `String(children)` (L83) and joins arrays (L81) with no HTML escaping. The header comment (L8-15) acknowledges this: 'Child strings are inserted as written ... SPEC.md section 4 does not yet define JSX text-escaping semantics.' This means any component that renders a user- or DB-derived string as a JSX child — `

{product.name}

`, `

{review.body}

`, `{username}` — emits that value verbatim into the HTML stream. I confirmed the compiler does NOT compensate: diffing the authored examples/commerce/src/components/product-grid.tsx against the lowered examples/commerce/src/generated/product-grid.tsx, the only injected change is the `fw-c`/`fw-deps` stamps; the dynamic text interpolations `

{item.id}

` and `

{item.stock} in stock

` are emitted identically and unescaped. The rendered fragment HTML is then delivered as `` (wire-html.ts renderFragmentWireHtml inserts options.html raw) and applied on the client via innerHTML (packages/runtime/src/morph.ts:28 `template.innerHTML = html.trim()`), so injected `
findings.recommendation: Escape text children by default. Introduce a distinct 'raw/trusted HTML' node type (or a `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`-style marker / a branded RawHtml string returned by framework helpers like csrfField and nested component renders), and run all other string children through escapeHtml in renderJsxChildren before concatenation. Pre-rendered framework HTML would opt into raw insertion explicitly; everything else (interpolated app data) is escaped. Alternatively have the compiler wrap every non-literal text expression in escapeHtml during lowering. Until then, document that JSX text is unescaped and require app authors to call escapeHtml manually.

findings.title: normalizePathname() preserves leading '//', yielding a protocol-relative redirect target (root cause of the app-request.ts open redirect)
findings.description: normalizePathname() ensures a leading slash and strips trailing slashes (`absolutePathname.replace(/\/+$/, '')`) but never collapses or rejects leading double-slashes. For input `//evil.com/` it returns `{ pathname: '//evil.com', redirect: { pathname: '//evil.com', status: 308 } }`. This redirect struct is consumed by handleAppRequest (app-request.ts:18-20), which places `//evil.com` directly into the Location header — a protocol-relative URL the browser resolves to https://evil.com (open redirect). This file is the natural fix site: the normalized pathname is treated as a trusted same-origin redirect target downstream, but it can encode an arbitrary host. Same chain as the app-request.ts finding; reported here because the unsanitized value originates in this function.
findings.recommendation: Collapse leading slashes to a single '/' (e.g. `absolutePathname.replace(/^\/+/, '/')`) or reject pathnames matching `^//` so that no normalized/redirect pathname can ever be protocol-relative. Add a regression test for `//evil.com/` and `/\evil.com/`.

findings.title: Idempotency/replay reservation placed after the handler runs — concurrent duplicates double-execute
findings.description: The replay store is designed to coalesce duplicate mutations (same FW-Idem + session scope): reserve() (L65-96) creates a pending record, get() (L50-64) returns `record.pending.then(...)` so in-flight duplicates wait for and reuse the first response instead of re-running. The defect is the ORDER in which the call-site uses it. In mutation.ts renderMutationResponse: (1) readMutationReplay (L409) does a NON-atomic get; (2) runMutation (L414) executes the handler WITH ALL ITS SIDE EFFECTS; (3) only then does withMutationReplay (L442/L453) call reserveMutationReplay → store.reserve, creating the pending record. So the pending/coalescing window does not cover the handler-execution phase, only the post-commit render phase. Two (or N) requests carrying the same FW-Idem and session that arrive while the first handler is still running both observe a get() miss (no record exists yet) and both proceed into runMutation — the handler executes multiple times. For a 'place order', 'charge', 'redeem single-use coupon', or 'transfer' mutation, that is duplicate orders / double charges / multi-redeem. An attacker controls FW-Idem and can deliberately fire simultaneous identical requests to defeat idempotency; it also fires on ordinary double-clicks and client retry storms. The replay.test.ts cases that 'replay duplicate requests while ... pending' only pass because they manually sequence the second call to start AFTER `await queryStarted/fragmentStarted` — i.e., after the handler finished and the reservation already exists — so they never exercise the handler-execution race. The reserve-returns-undefined fallback in commitMutationReplay (mutation path) also silently overwrites via set() rather than returning the in-flight result, compounding the issue.
findings.recommendation: Reserve the idempotency key atomically at the SPEC §9.4 'replay lookup' step, before running the handler. Replace the separate read-then-run with a single get-or-reserve: if a record already exists (pending or committed), await/return it without re-running the handler; otherwise atomically create the pending reservation, run the handler+render under it, and commit. This makes the pending-promise coalescing cover handler execution so concurrent duplicates collapse to a single execution. (In mutation.ts, move reserveMutationReplay ahead of runMutation and feed the pending result back to duplicates.)

findings.title: File MIME allowlist trusts the client-declared content type
findings.description: FileSchema.mime()/parseFileLike validate the upload against `input.type` (L334: `options.mime.includes(input.type)`), which is the MIME type declared by the client in the multipart part headers — fully attacker-controlled and unrelated to the actual bytes. An attacker can upload an HTML/SVG/script payload while setting `Content-Type: image/png` to pass a `mime(['image/png'])` allowlist. The size check (L331) is sound, but the type check provides a false sense of safety. The stored object's contentType is then derived from the same untrusted `file.type` (StoredFileSchemaImpl.parse L291 / parseAsync L308). If the application later serves uploads inline from a same-origin path (common for avatars/attachments) and trusts that stored content type, this enables stored XSS or content-sniffing attacks. This is a defense-in-depth gap inherent to client-declared MIME; the framework exposes mime() as if it were a security control.
findings.recommendation: Treat client-declared MIME as untrusted: sniff the actual content (magic-byte detection) and validate against the allowlist, or at minimum document that mime() only checks the claimed type. When serving uploads, force a safe Content-Type, send `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, prefer `Content-Disposition: attachment` for non-image types, and serve user uploads from a separate origin/sandbox.

findings.title: Synchronous StoredFileSchema.parse returns a 'stored' upload without persisting the file
findings.description: StoredFileSchemaImpl.parse (L277-299, the synchronous Schema.parse) computes the storage key and builds a StoredFileUpload with a populated `storage` StorageObjectInfo, but never calls storage.put — only the async parseAsync (L301-316) actually persists the bytes. If a stored-file schema is ever validated through the synchronous path (e.g. `s.object({...}).parse(...)`, which dispatches to nested `schema.parse`), the caller receives a result implying the file was stored when it was not, leading to silent data loss / dangling references (DB rows pointing at objects that don't exist). The mutation pipeline currently uses parseSchemaAsync (which prefers parseAsync), so the main path is safe, but the synchronous Schema interface makes the unsafe path reachable and easy to hit in app or test code.
findings.recommendation: Make synchronous parse of a storing schema fail fast (throw 'stored file requires async parsing') instead of returning an unstored StoredFileUpload, or have it not claim storage info it didn't create. Ensure all framework entry points that may receive stored-file schemas use parseSchemaAsync/parseAsync.

findings.title: Skip-mode export silently drops valid concrete URLs of a param route when a sibling staticPath is non-exportable
findings.description: NOT a security issue (fails safe — it over-skips, never over-exposes). staticExportParamRouteTargets emits both diagnostics and targets keyed by the route *pattern* (route.path) rather than by the concrete target path: a malformed/duplicate/unsafe staticPath pushes staticExportDiagnostic(route.path, ...), while a valid sibling staticPath pushes a target with { routePath: route.path }. In replayStaticExportApp (static-export-replay.ts) under onNonExportable:'skip', the per-target filter is `diagnostics.some(d => d.routePath === routeTarget.routePath)`. Because the diagnostic and the valid target share the same routePath (the pattern), a single bad staticPath causes ALL valid concrete URLs of that route to be skipped. Example: route '/products/:id' with staticPaths ['/products/p1', '/products/%2f'] — '/products/%2f' is correctly rejected as unsafe, but '/products/p1' (valid, safe, exportable) is then also silently omitted from the export, defeating the purpose of 'skip' mode. The root enabler (pattern-keyed diagnostics) lives in this file; the faulty filter line is in static-export-replay.ts. In the default (non-skip) mode this is harmless because any diagnostic aborts the whole export.
findings.recommendation: Key the skip decision by concrete target path, not route pattern: either have route-plan associate diagnostics with the specific concrete path (e.g. add a targetPath field), or change the replay filter to skip only targets whose concrete path failed (track failed concrete paths in a Set) rather than excluding every target that shares a route pattern with any diagnostic. Add a test for a param route with mixed valid/invalid staticPaths under onNonExportable:'skip' asserting the valid URL is still exported.

findings.title: Stored XSS: autocomplete option/value text rendered unescaped into innerHTML sink
findings.description: AutocompleteOption renders `{props.children ?? props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue}` (line 254) and AutocompleteValue renders `{autocompleteValueText(props)}` (line 284). `autocompleteValueText` (packages/headless-ui/src/primitives/autocomplete.ts:164-169) returns raw item data (`selected.label ?? selected.textValue ?? selected.value`, or the current `value`/`placeholder`). These are scalar DATA props, not composition slots. The server JSX runtime does NOT escape child text — `renderJsxChildren` does `return String(children)` (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:83), with a comment acknowledging text-escaping is an unresolved open question. The rendered HTML string is sent in the initial document and in mutation/query wire fragments, which the client applies via `insertAdjacentHTML`/`innerHTML` (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts:58,62) — threat-model stored-XSS vector #3. Because autocomplete/combobox item lists are normally sourced from the database (e.g. usernames, product names, saved searches; the gallery demo uses people's names), an attacker who controls an item label/value (or the persisted selected value) injects markup such as `` that executes in every viewer's browser. Proof that this is an oversight, not a 'caller pre-escapes' contract: the same `itemValue` is emitted as an auto-escaped attribute (`value={attrs.value}`, line 179) AND as an unescaped child — the caller cannot pre-escape without breaking the attribute, so the component must escape the child itself and currently does not. No UI component escapes child text (only table.tsx escapes, and only attributes via escapeAttribute).
findings.recommendation: Escape user/DB-derived text before placing it in child position. Either (a) fix the root cause by having the JSX runtime HTML-escape string children in renderJsxChildren (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts) while keeping an explicit raw-HTML escape hatch for intentional composition, or (b) in these components wrap data-derived text with escapeHtml (from @jiso/server) before interpolating — e.g. `{props.children ?? escapeHtml(props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue)}` and `{escapeHtml(autocompleteValueText(props))}`. Resolving the framework-level text-escaping open question is the durable fix.

findings.title: Stored XSS: combobox option/value text rendered unescaped into innerHTML sink
findings.description: ComboboxOption renders `{props.children ?? props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue}` (line 248) and ComboboxValue renders `{comboboxValueText(props)}` (line 277). `comboboxValueText` (packages/headless-ui/src/primitives/combobox.ts:143-148) returns raw item data (`selected.label ?? selected.textValue ?? selected.value`, or the current `value`/`placeholder`). These scalar DATA props are placed in child position, and the server JSX runtime does not escape child text (`return String(children)`, packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:83). The resulting HTML is delivered in the initial document and in wire fragments applied client-side via `insertAdjacentHTML`/`innerHTML` (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts:58,62), matching threat-model stored-XSS #3. Combobox item lists are typically DB-sourced (the gallery demo binds them to people's names); an attacker controlling a label/value (e.g. a chosen display name or product title) injects executing markup like `<svg onload=...>` into every viewer's page. The bug is an oversight, not a contract: `itemValue` is emitted as an auto-escaped attribute (`value={attrs.value}`, line 246) AND as an unescaped child, so the caller cannot pre-escape it without corrupting the attribute — the component must escape the child and does not. Note the option element here is a `<div role=option>` (line 237), so injected markup renders directly (unlike a native `<option>`).
findings.recommendation: Escape DB/user-derived text in child position. Preferred: fix renderJsxChildren in packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts to HTML-escape string children with an explicit raw-HTML opt-out for composition. Otherwise, in this component wrap data text with escapeHtml (from @jiso/server): `{props.children ?? escapeHtml(props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue)}` and `{escapeHtml(comboboxValueText(props))}`. Resolve the framework's tracked text-escaping open question to fix this class of bug everywhere.

findings.title: Stored XSS: command value and item label/value rendered as unescaped HTML text
findings.description: CommandValue renders `{commandValueText(props)}` (L369) and CommandItem renders `{props.children ?? props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue}` (L316) as element text children. `commandValueText` (packages/headless-ui/src/primitives/command.ts:299-303) returns `selected.label ?? selected.textValue ?? selected.value` or `state.value` — i.e. data drawn from `props.items`/`props.value`. The @jiso/server JSX runtime does NOT escape text children: `renderJsxChildren` (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:79-84) returns `String(children)` verbatim (only attributes are escaped, jsx-runtime.ts:66). Command palettes are routinely populated from DB/user data (file names, product titles, search results). When a mutation/route renders this component into a wire fragment, the runtime applies it via `innerHTML`/`insertAdjacentHTML` (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts:58,62), so an item label like `` executes. This is the 'unescaped fragment HTML / stored XSS' risk called out in the project threat model. NOTE: the scanner's insecure-crypto flags on L51/64/206/212/232/240/243 are false positives — they match the substring 'des' in descriptionId/aria-describedby/aria-activedescendant, not a DES cipher.
findings.recommendation: Escape scalar text values before interpolating: render `{escapeHtml(commandValueText(props))}` and `{escapeHtml(props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue)}` (escapeHtml is exported from @jiso/server, see packages/server/src/html.ts). Better, fix the root cause: make `renderJsxChildren` escape string children by default and provide an explicit raw-HTML/`children`-is-prerendered opt-in, since today every component that emits text is a latent XSS sink.

findings.title: Potential XSS: dialog content/trigger children rendered as unescaped HTML
findings.description: DialogContent (L140), DialogTrigger (L116) and DialogClose (L164) interpolate `{props.children}` as element content. Because the @jiso/server JSX runtime inserts text children verbatim (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:79-84, no escaping; attributes only are escaped at line 66) and fragments are applied client-side via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts:58,62), any raw dynamic string passed as a dialog body — e.g. `<DialogContent>{userMessage}</DialogContent>` — executes injected markup. `children` is typed `string` (DialogProps.children?: string), which invites passing dynamic data. This is the same systemic unescaped-text-children gap as the other components; confidence is lower than the scalar-prop cases because `children` is the framework's intended composition slot for pre-rendered HTML, so exploitability depends on the app passing an unescaped raw string rather than nested components. The scanner's insecure-crypto flags (L35/127/133) are false positives matching 'des' in descriptionId/aria-describedby.
findings.recommendation: Decide and document a text-escaping contract for JSX children. Either escape string children by default in the runtime (with an explicit opt-in for trusted pre-rendered HTML), or require callers to escape dynamic strings and document that DialogContent children are treated as raw HTML. Until then, treat any dynamic dialog body as an XSS sink.

findings.title: Stored XSS: Drawer title and description rendered as unescaped HTML text
findings.description:
The Drawer component interpolates the scalar string props `{props.title}` into `

` (L107) and `{props.description}` into `

` (L113). `title` is a required string (DrawerProps.title, L25) so it is always rendered. The @jiso/server JSX runtime does not escape text children — `renderJsxChildren` returns `String(children)` verbatim (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:79-84); only attributes are escaped (line 66). These are scalar props the component itself converts into raw HTML, so the caller has no opportunity to control escaping. A drawer titled with user/DB-derived data (a saved-address label, document name, product title) containing `
findings.recommendation:
Escape the text props in the component: `

{escapeHtml(props.title)}

` and `{escapeHtml(props.description)}` (escapeHtml from @jiso/server, packages/server/src/html.ts). Long term, make the JSX runtime escape string children by default so scalar text props are safe without per-component escaping.

findings.title: Potential XSS: field message / option / textarea children rendered as unescaped HTML
findings.description: FieldError (L400), FieldDescription (L374), FieldSelectOption (L349), FieldTextarea (L300) and FieldLabel (L213) interpolate `{props.children}` directly as element text content. The @jiso/server runtime inserts these verbatim with no escaping (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts:79-84; attributes only are escaped, line 66), and fragments reach the DOM via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts:58,62). Validation error text and select-option labels are commonly derived from user/DB input (e.g. an error like `"<script>" is not a valid email`), so a raw dynamic string passed as children executes. Note `value`/`placeholder` go into attributes and ARE escaped, so those are safe; the risk is the text-content children. Confidence is medium because `children` is the framework's composition slot (intended to carry pre-rendered HTML), so the precise sink is shared with the caller's `{msg}` interpolation. The scanner's insecure-crypto flags (L7/38/56/71/97/140/166/223/239/267/282/309/321/355/357/367/409/421) are false positives matching 'des' in description/descriptionId/aria-describedby/fieldDescription.
findings.recommendation: For message/label/option components that semantically carry text, escape children (`{escapeHtml(props.children)}`) or expose a separate escaped `text`/`message` prop. Preferred root-cause fix: have the JSX runtime escape string children by default with an explicit raw-HTML opt-in for true composition cases.

findings.title: MenubarItem renders itemLabel/itemValue as unescaped text (stored XSS sink)
findings.description: In MenubarItem (line 168) the fallback chain `{props.children ?? props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue}` emits a scalar string prop as a JSX text child. The @jiso/server JSX runtime does NOT escape text children (jsx-runtime.ts:83 returns `String(children)`; the L12-15 comment confirms child strings are 'inserted as written'). Unlike attribute values, which ARE escaped (jsx-runtime.ts:66), `itemLabel` and `itemValue` are written into the element body verbatim. `itemValue` is a required prop and is semantically a plain-text label/value, not pre-rendered HTML, so a developer would not expect to escape it. If an app populates a menubar from user/DB data (e.g. saved-search names, product categories), a value like `` is emitted raw. Because Jiso mutation responses are server-rendered HTML fragments applied client-side via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML, and the initial SSR HTML is also raw, this is stored XSS (threat-model attack #3). NOTE: this is a framework-wide characteristic of the unescaped-text-children design (an open question per the runtime comment and the v1 cleanup ledger), not a defect unique to this file; the bare `{props.children}` composition slots elsewhere are by-design and not flagged. Confidence is low because exploitability depends on the consuming app routing user data into itemLabel/itemValue.
findings.recommendation: Escape scalar text props before emitting them as children — e.g. render `{props.children ?? escapeHtml(props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue)}` (escapeHtml from @jiso/server), or resolve the framework's open JSX text-escaping decision so the runtime escapes string children by default while preserving an explicit raw-HTML opt-in for composition.

findings.title: NavigationMenuTrigger/Link render itemLabel/itemValue as unescaped text (stored XSS sink)
findings.description: NavigationMenuTrigger (line 229) and NavigationMenuLink (line 284) both emit `{props.children ?? props.itemLabel ?? props.itemValue}`. As confirmed in jsx-runtime.ts:83, text children are NOT escaped (only attributes are, jsx-runtime.ts:66). `itemLabel`/`itemValue` are scalar string props meant as plain-text labels, so HTML metacharacters in user/DB-derived values are emitted raw into the `<button>`/`<a>` body. Rendered via the server fragment pipeline (applied client-side through innerHTML) or initial SSR, this is stored XSS when an app builds nav items from user-controlled data. Same framework-wide unescaped-text-children root cause as menubar.tsx; confidence low because it depends on app data flow.
findings.recommendation: Escape itemLabel/itemValue before emitting them as text children (e.g. escapeHtml from @jiso/server), or fix the runtime's default text-escaping so string children are HTML-escaped with an explicit opt-in for pre-rendered HTML composition.

findings.title: NavigationMenuLink passes href through without URL-scheme validation (javascript: URI XSS)
findings.description: NavigationMenuLink renders `href={attrs.href}` (line 279), where `attrs.href` comes from `props.href` passed verbatim by navigationMenuLinkAttributes (headless-ui/src/primitives/navigation-menu.ts:232 — `{ href: options.href }`, no validation). The href is escaped as an attribute, but escapeAttribute (html.ts:5) only encodes `& < > "` — it does NOT neutralize dangerous URL schemes. A user-controlled href of `javascript:alert(document.cookie)` (or a `data:`/`vbscript:` URI) therefore survives intact and executes on click. There is no allowlist or `http(s):`/relative-path check anywhere in the component or the headless helper. This is a classic href-injection XSS that lands whenever an app feeds user-controlled URLs into a navigation link. Confidence low because it requires the app to supply an attacker-influenced href.
findings.recommendation: Validate the href scheme before rendering — reject or neutralize anything that is not an allowlisted scheme (http, https, mailto, tel) or a relative/anchor path (e.g. starts with `/`, `#`, `./`). Implement the check in navigationMenuLinkAttributes (or the component) and drop/replace disallowed hrefs with `#` or omit the attribute.

findings.title: Hardcoded CSRF HMAC secret in source (tutorial scaffolding)
findings.description: `shopCsrf.secret` is the hardcoded literal 'tutorial-shop-secret' (L32). CSRF tokens are an HMAC over the session id keyed by this secret; a hardcoded/known secret lets an attacker forge valid CSRF tokens and defeat the default-on CSRF protection on the `cart/add` mutation. This is almost certainly intentional tutorial scaffolding (functionally identical to the documented `EXAMPLE_ONLY_*_CSRF_SECRET` known-false-positives, just not labeled that way) and is only a real risk if copied verbatim into a deployed app. Flagged at low confidence for that reason. The L96 'template literal in HTML' and L63/L150 'db call without await' flags are false positives: renderShopPage interpolates only fixed seed data and JSX-auto-escaped fragment output, and `request.db.products.get` is a synchronous Map.get, not an async DB call.
findings.recommendation: For production use, load the CSRF secret from an environment variable / secret manager rather than a source literal, and rename the tutorial constant to the documented EXAMPLE_ONLY_* convention so scanners and readers treat it as a fixture.

findings.title: Unescaped DB string ({item.name}) rendered as JSX child in an innerHTML-applied fragment
findings.description: The @jiso/server JSX runtime escapes attribute values but NOT text children: renderJsxChildren() returns String(children) with no escaping (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts L79-84; the L12-15 comment notes text-escaping is undefined). In ProductList.render (L33) the free-form DB string `{item.name}` (ShopProduct.name) is interpolated as a child, so it is emitted raw. ProductList is fragmentTarget:true and is rendered into `` for mutation responses and the deferred stream (app.ts L94, L130, L176, L208); the client applies that fragment HTML via element.innerHTML / insertAdjacentHTML with no sanitization (packages/runtime/src/response-fragment-apply.ts L58, L62). Any markup in a product name (e.g. ``) therefore becomes stored XSS executed in the victim's browser. This is the exact 'unescaped fragment HTML' pattern called out in the project threat model. It is NOT directly exploitable in this tutorial because product names are static fixtures and the addToCart mutation never writes a name from user input (it spreads ...found), so the data is not currently attacker-controlled — hence MEDIUM/medium confidence. It rises to HIGH the moment `name` is sourced from admin/vendor/user input (the normal case for a real catalog). Note the reference app examples/commerce/product-grid.tsx deliberately avoids this: it renders only {item.id}/{item.stock} as children and uses escapeHtml/escapeAttribute for free-form data (examples/commerce/src/app.ts L694, L804, L839). The scanner's insecure-crypto flag on L16/L47 is a false positive: those are comment lines, and the only crypto reachable here is HMAC-SHA256 + timingSafeEqual in csrf.ts, which is correct.
findings.recommendation: Escape user/DB strings rendered as JSX children, e.g. `{escapeHtml(item.name)}` (packages/server/src/html.ts), matching the examples/commerce pattern. Better, fix the framework root cause so the JSX runtime auto-escapes string text children (renderJsxChildren) while preserving a branded/raw type for pre-rendered HTML such as component renders and csrfField output; this closes the footgun for all components rather than relying on every author to remember.

findings.title: Non-atomic read-modify-write in transaction() can lose concurrent updates
findings.description: transaction() clones the database (cloneShopDb(db)), `await`s the handler (run(draft)), then commits by wholesale-replacing db.cartItems and db.products with the draft's copies. The `await` is an event-loop yield point. If two mutations execute concurrently against the *same* ShopDb instance, both clone the same base snapshot, each mutates its own draft, and the later commit overwrites the earlier one entirely (last-writer-wins) — the classic lost-update race / non-atomic read-modify-write. Within this tutorial the impact is limited and the confidence is low because request.db is never shown to be a shared, persistent instance (renderShopPage defaults to a fresh createShopDb() and tests construct a fresh db per case), so the race is latent in the transaction primitive rather than active here. It is worth noting because learners may reuse this transaction shape against a persistent store. This is a correctness bug, not a security vulnerability — the cart/products are global demo state with no per-user scoping to violate.
findings.recommendation: If the in-memory db is ever shared across concurrent requests, serialize transactions (an async mutex/queue around clone+commit) or perform optimistic-version / per-row checks at commit so concurrent writers cannot clobber each other. The SPEC's blessed Drizzle adapter provides real transactional isolation; the tutorial could note that this in-memory primitive intentionally omits concurrency control.

findings.title: DB strings rendered as JSX text children are not HTML-escaped (latent stored XSS)
findings.description: renderShopPage assembles server fragments (L223-227) from component renders that interpolate DB-derived strings as JSX text children: OrderHistory renders `{item.productId}` (generated/order-history.tsx L20) and ProductList renders `{item.name}` (generated/product-list.tsx L29). The server JSX runtime escapes ATTRIBUTE values but does NOT escape text children — renderJsxChildren returns `String(children)` raw (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts L79-84), and its own comment notes 'SPEC.md section 4 does not yet define JSX text-escaping semantics.' These fragments are applied on the client via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML, so any HTML-bearing string in a text position is executed. CLAUDE.md's threat model explicitly classifies unescaped user/DB strings in fragments as stored XSS. In THIS tutorial the exploit is currently blocked: the mutation only writes an order after `request.db.products.get(input.productId)` succeeds (L128-138), so `orders[].productId` is constrained to existing keys p1/p2/p3, and product names are static, and qty/total/count are numbers. So I rate concrete exploitability low — but this is the exact pattern (DB string -> JSX text child -> innerHTML) that becomes stored XSS the moment any rendered field (product name/description, order label) is attacker-influenced, as it would be in a real commerce app. The tutorial teaches the unsafe pattern.
findings.recommendation: Have the JSX runtime escape text children by default (escapeHtml in renderJsxChildren) with an explicit opt-out wrapper for trusted pre-rendered HTML, OR have components run DB/user strings through escapeHtml before interpolation (e.g. `{escapeHtml(item.name)}`, `{escapeHtml(item.productId)}`). Resolve the open text-escaping question in SPEC §4 so the safe default is guaranteed.

findings.title: Hardcoded CSRF HMAC secret in source
findings.description: shopCsrf.secret = 'tutorial-shop-secret' (L62) is a hardcoded secret used as the HMAC-SHA256 key for CSRF tokens over the session id. A hardcoded, source-committed CSRF secret means tokens are forgeable by anyone who can read the repo. This is almost certainly an intentional tutorial fixture analogous to the documented known-false-positives (`EXAMPLE_ONLY_*_CSRF_SECRET`), so I rate it low confidence and likely a non-issue in context — flagging only so the value is never lifted into a real deployment.
findings.recommendation: If this pattern is ever copied to production, source the CSRF secret from an environment variable/secret store, not a literal. Consider renaming to an EXAMPLE_ONLY_* convention to mark it unmistakably as a fixture.

findings.title: Unescaped DB string {item.name} rendered as a JSX text child into an innerHTML-applied fragment (latent stored XSS)
findings.description: The render function interpolates the product name as a JSX text child: `{item.name} — {formatPrice(item.unitPrice)} ({item.stock} in stock)` (L28). The @jiso/server JSX runtime escapes ATTRIBUTE values (escapeAttribute in renderJsxAttributes) but does NOT escape text CHILDREN — renderJsxChildren returns `String(children)` verbatim (packages/server/src/jsx-runtime.ts L79-84, self-documented as an unresolved escaping question). The output of this component is wrapped in <fw-fragment target="product-list"> (app.ts renderShopPage/submitAddToCart) and applied on the client via innerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML (runtime/response-fragment-apply.ts, morph.ts per the project threat model), so any HTML in item.name would execute as stored XSS. item.name is DB data (ShopProduct.name), and the project's own convention (CLAUDE.md) requires DB strings reaching a fragment to pass through escapeHtml/escapeAttribute. This file does neither. IMPORTANT — NOT CURRENTLY EXPLOITABLE: in this tutorial app product names are static seed values ('Pour-over kettle', etc., db.ts L46-48) and there is no attacker-reachable write path for a name — the only mutation (cart/add) writes products via `{ ...found, stock: ... }` (app.ts L145-148), preserving the existing name; there are no other mutations, endpoints, or webhooks. This is therefore a latent/defense-in-depth weakness: it becomes a real stored XSS the moment product names become user/admin-editable (the norm for a commerce app). Notably the reference examples/commerce/product-grid.tsx deliberately renders only {item.id} and {item.stock} (its card item type omits `name` entirely), which is the safer pattern this tutorial diverges from. As teaching material, it propagates an insecure rendering habit.
findings.recommendation: Escape DB/user free-text before it becomes a JSX text child, e.g. import escapeHtml from @jiso/server and render `{escapeHtml(item.name)}`, or mirror the reference commerce component by not rendering free-form text children. Longer term, resolve the framework-level open question by making the @jiso/server JSX runtime auto-escape text children (with an explicit raw/dangerous opt-in for pre-rendered HTML) so app authors are secure by default.

findings.title: Transaction commit overwrites whole tables (lost update under concurrency)
findings.description: The in-memory transaction clones the db, runs the handler on the draft, then commits by wholesale reassignment: `db.cartItems = draft.cartItems; db.orders = draft.orders; db.products = draft.products` (L54-56). Two transactions that interleave (each cloning the same base, then committing) would clobber each other's writes — last-writer-wins on the entire table rather than row-level merge. The handlers here are synchronous so no interleaving occurs within a single event-loop turn, and the tutorial uses per-request in-memory stubs, so this is not exploitable as wired; noting it as a latent correctness footgun in the reference transaction pattern. The scanner's missing-await flags on L62/L65/L69 are false positives: Array.push and Map.set are synchronous.
findings.recommendation: For any persistence behind this interface, use real transactional isolation (or row/version-level merge) rather than swapping whole table references on commit. No action needed for the in-memory tutorial stub.

findings.title: Order-history query returns all users' orders with no per-user scoping or guard
findings.description: loadOrderHistory returns `db.orders` in full (L38-40) and orderHistoryQuery (L57-61) declares no guard and no filter by the session user. Orders are written with a per-user `userId` (app.ts L143), and the session schema carries `user.id` (app.ts L51-58), so order history is user-scoped data — but the read returns every order regardless of who is asking. CLAUDE.md's IDOR guidance requires handlers/queries that read user-scoped rows to filter by `request.session.user.id`; this query does not. In the tutorial this does not leak because the home route builds a fresh per-call in-memory db (app.ts L212, L232) so orders are empty there, and tests use a single user — hence low confidence. But against a shared/persistent `request.db` (as the mutation response path uses, app.ts L286), every authenticated user would see all users' order history (userId, productId, qty, total). This is the broken-access-control / cross-tenant pattern, deviating from the user-scoped intent the data model implies. Scanner missing-await flags on L29/L34 are false positives (Array.reduce / Array.sort are synchronous).
findings.recommendation: Scope order history to the authenticated user: pass the session into the query load context and filter `db.orders.filter(o => o.userId === context.request.session.user.id)`, and attach an `authed`/session guard to orderHistoryQuery so it cannot be read unauthenticated.
